subject: Engineering
description: Formulated the initial research proposal to investigate novel signal processing techniques for early fault detection in rotating machinery. The project aims to develop a method robust to non-stationary operating conditions, such as varying speeds and loads.
reason: As the first formal step, this event translates a broad, pre-existing research idea into a concrete set of `research_goals` and an initial `research_plan`. The requirement for `research_goals` reflects the abstract project concept that serves as the input to this formalization process.

subject: Engineering
description: Designed the initial methodological scheme. The plan is to use a test rig with a variable-speed motor and interchangeable bearings (healthy, inner race fault, outer race fault). Vibration data will be captured using accelerometers, and the proposed signal processing pipeline will involve Continuous Wavelet Transform (CWT) for time-frequency analysis, followed by a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) for fault classification.
reason: Following the high-level proposal in e_01, this step translates the abstract `research_goals` into a concrete technical plan. Designing the `method_scheme` is a necessary prerequisite for setting up the hardware and running any pilot experiments to test the feasibility of the approach.

subject: Engineering
description: Conducted pilot experiments using the designed test rig. We collected vibration data for two conditions: a healthy bearing and a bearing with a simulated outer race fault, both operating at a constant speed. The goal was to verify the data acquisition setup and perform a preliminary check on whether the fault signatures are discernible in the raw time-frequency plots.
reason: With the `method_scheme` from e_02 defined, a pilot study is the logical next step. This small-scale experiment allows for validation of the experimental setup and provides initial data to test the feasibility of the proposed analysis pipeline before committing resources to the full `main_experiments`.

subject: Engineering
description: Analyzed the data from the pilot experiments. Applying the Continuous Wavelet Transform (CWT) to the vibration signals revealed distinct patterns in the time-frequency domain, clearly differentiating the healthy bearing from the one with an outer race fault. This initial analysis confirms that the chosen signal processing technique is capable of extracting relevant fault features.
reason: After collecting initial data in the pilot study (e_03), a formal analysis is the logical next step to validate the approach. This event uses the `method_scheme` from e_02 to process the raw `experiment_results` and confirms the feasibility of the feature extraction method before proceeding to more complex experiments or model training.

subject: Engineering
description: Began the main experimental phase based on the successful pilot study. Data collection was expanded to include all three bearing conditions (healthy, inner race fault, outer race fault) under non-stationary operating conditions, specifically variable-speed ramps. This comprehensive dataset is necessary to train and validate the proposed fault detection model against the project's core objectives.
reason: The successful analysis of pilot data (e_04) validated the data acquisition and feature extraction approach for a simple case. The project now progresses to the main experiments, as outlined in the `research_plan`, to collect data under the more challenging variable-speed conditions. This step is crucial for addressing the primary research goal of robustness to non-stationary operation.

subject: Engineering
description: Applied the full analysis pipeline to the main experimental dataset. The CWT-generated time-frequency images were used to train the Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) to classify the three bearing conditions under variable speeds. The model achieved a promising, but not perfect, classification accuracy of 85% on the test set, with some confusion between the healthy and inner race fault conditions.
reason: After collecting the comprehensive dataset in the main experiments (e_05), the crucial next step is to perform the full analysis outlined in the `method_scheme`. This involves training and evaluating the CNN model on the challenging non-stationary data, which directly tests the core hypothesis of the project. The results provide the first quantitative performance measure of the entire system.

subject: Engineering
description: While considering how to improve the model's performance, the team begins drafting the manuscript. The initial draft focuses on the 'Methods' section, detailing the test rig setup, the data acquisition protocol for both constant and variable speeds, and the CWT-CNN analysis pipeline as it was implemented.
reason: The analysis in e_06 produced a significant, albeit imperfect, result. It is a common and efficient practice in research to begin drafting the methods section of a paper at this stage, as the experimental procedure is now well-defined. This can be done in parallel with diagnosing the model's weaknesses and planning improvements.

subject: Engineering
description: The analysis in e_06 revealed that the model struggles to differentiate inner race faults from healthy bearings under variable speeds. To address this, the methodological scheme is revised to incorporate order tracking as a signal pre-processing step. This will resample the data with respect to the shaft's angle rather than time, aiming to stabilize the fault signatures during speed variations.
reason: The 85% accuracy result from the previous analysis (e_06) is promising but not sufficient, with a specific, identified weakness. A standard iterative research process involves diagnosing and fixing such issues. Revising the `method_scheme` is a direct response to the unsatisfactory `experiment_results` and represents a targeted attempt to improve the core method before proceeding with further analysis or writing.

subject: Engineering
description: The main experimental dataset was re-analyzed using the revised method. Order tracking was applied to the raw vibration signals before CWT, and the CNN was retrained on the new set of time-frequency images. The new approach yielded a significantly improved classification accuracy of 96%, successfully distinguishing the inner race fault from the healthy bearing condition under variable speeds.
reason: Having revised the `method_scheme` in e_08 to address a specific performance issue, the immediate and necessary next step is to apply this new scheme to the existing data. This analysis validates the proposed improvement (order tracking) by directly re-evaluating the model's performance on the challenging dataset from the `main_experiments`, confirming the fix before proceeding.

subject: Engineering
description: With the revised method validated and showing high accuracy, the focus shifts back to the manuscript. The 'Methods' section is updated to include the critical order tracking pre-processing step, and a new 'Results' section is drafted, presenting the 96% classification accuracy, confusion matrices, and visualizations of the CWT images.
reason: The successful re-analysis in e_09 produced the final, strong results for the project. The immediate next step is to incorporate these findings and the revised methodology from e_08 into the paper draft that was started in e_07, advancing the project towards completion and publication.

subject: Engineering
description: The team drafts the remaining sections of the paper. The 'Introduction' is written to frame the problem of fault diagnosis under non-stationary conditions, and the 'Conclusion' summarizes the key findings, emphasizing the 96% accuracy and the importance of order tracking. The 'Discussion' section is also added to interpret the results and suggest avenues for future work.
reason: With the core methods and results sections (e_10) drafted based on the successful analysis (e_09), completing the manuscript is the next logical step. This involves writing the introduction to motivate the work based on the original `research_goals` and the conclusion to summarize the strong `experiment_results`, moving the project closer to submission.

subject: Engineering
description: Following internal review of the complete paper draft, the team decides to expand the project's scope before submission. The current results are strong for fault classification, but the 'Discussion' section highlights the potential for prognosis. The research goals are officially revised to include the prediction of Remaining Useful Life (RUL) of the bearings, which will require a new long-term degradation experiment.
reason: The project has successfully met its initial goals (e_09) and a full paper draft exists (e_11). It is common at this stage to identify major extensions that would significantly increase the impact of the work. Revisiting the `proposal` to expand from diagnosis to prognosis is a logical next step for a long-term project, building on the validated feature extraction method to tackle a more complex problem.

subject: Engineering
description: To address the new goal of predicting Remaining Useful Life (RUL), the team designs a new experimental scheme. This involves a long-term endurance test where a bearing will run continuously under a constant load until failure, with vibration data collected at regular intervals. The analysis scheme is also revised to use the CWT features as input to a Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) network, which is better suited for time-series forecasting than a CNN.
reason: After the project's scope was expanded in e_12 to include RUL prediction, the existing `method_scheme` for classification is no longer sufficient. This event is the necessary next step to design a new experimental and analytical framework specifically for the new prognostic goal, translating the revised `research_goals` into a concrete technical plan.

subject: Engineering
description: The team commences the new long-term endurance experiment as designed. A new bearing is installed in the test rig and run continuously under constant load and speed. Vibration data is captured automatically every hour to build a comprehensive time-series dataset that documents the bearing's degradation from a healthy state to eventual failure.
reason: With the new experimental protocol for RUL prediction designed in e_13, the direct next step is to execute it and collect the necessary data. This `main_experiments` phase is critical as it generates the long-term degradation dataset required to train the LSTM model for the new prognostic goal, directly following the revised `research_plan`.

subject: Engineering
description: The endurance test has been running for several weeks, and the collected vibration data is showing a clear upward trend in certain frequency bands, indicating progressive wear. A preliminary analysis is conducted on this partial dataset to train the initial LSTM model for RUL prediction. The model shows it can learn the degradation trend, but its predictions are still highly variable.
reason: After starting a long-term experiment (e_14), it is common and efficient to perform intermediate analysis on the partial data. This step uses the RUL `method_scheme` (e_13) to process the ongoing `experiment_results`, allowing the team to verify that the degradation is being captured and to begin developing the prognostic model before the experiment is complete.

subject: Engineering
description: To combat the high variability in RUL predictions observed in the preliminary analysis, the analysis scheme is refined. The team decides to incorporate an attention mechanism into the LSTM network. The hypothesis is that this will allow the model to dynamically focus on the most salient features of the degradation signal over time, leading to more stable and accurate predictions.
reason: The previous analysis (e_15) identified unstable predictions as a key issue with the current RUL model. This event represents a targeted effort to improve the model's architecture based on those findings. Revising the `method_scheme` is a necessary step to address this weakness before running a final analysis on the complete dataset.

subject: Engineering
description: The long-term endurance experiment concluded with bearing failure, providing a complete degradation dataset. The team applied the revised analysis scheme, training the LSTM with an attention mechanism on the full dataset. The new model demonstrated significantly more stable and accurate RUL predictions, confirming the benefit of the attention mechanism.
reason: Following the refinement of the prognostic model in e_16, this analysis applies the new method to the complete degradation dataset from the endurance test (e_14). This step is crucial to validate whether the proposed attention mechanism successfully resolves the prediction instability issue identified in the preliminary analysis (e_15).

subject: Engineering
description: The team integrates the successful RUL prediction findings into the manuscript. This involves rewriting the introduction to cover both diagnosis and prognosis, expanding the methods to include the endurance test and the attention-LSTM model, and adding a new results section detailing the RUL prediction performance. The conclusion is updated to summarize the dual contributions of the project.
reason: With the successful RUL analysis in e_17, the project has achieved all its revised goals. The immediate priority is to update the manuscript to include this major new contribution, creating a comprehensive paper that covers both the diagnostic and prognostic aspects of the research.

subject: Engineering
description: The comprehensive manuscript, which now includes both the high-accuracy fault diagnosis method and the novel RUL prediction results, has been finalized after internal review. It is submitted for peer review to a leading journal in mechanical systems and signal processing. This concludes the primary research and development phase of the project.
reason: The project has successfully met its initial and expanded research goals (e_12), with strong validated results for both fault diagnosis (e_09) and prognosis (e_17). A complete manuscript has been drafted and revised (e_18) to reflect all contributions. Submitting the work for publication represents the culmination of the research cycle, making this a natural point to conclude the project timeline.
